speaker: interviewer
text: Survivor is Benjamin Jacobs. The date is July 16, 1996. We left off on the last tape talking about going into the ghetto. I asked you if you knew the name of the commandant or the officer in charge of the ghetto. Do you know who that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Schweickert, was Schweickert. S-C-H-W-E-I-K-E-R-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you know his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe him, first of all? Did you know anything about him personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had no personal contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can only describe him as being [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] unavailable to any Jew. Anything that he wanted he [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] ordered the Judenrat to function in his behalf.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Judenrat-- head of the Judenrat was Mordha Francus, F-R-A-N-C-U-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know him at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mordha Francus I knew very well, yes. He was one of the Jews that lived in Dobra and friend of-- well, not my friend, but certainly a friend of my father and my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the conditions of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Conditions of the ghetto were extremely difficult. We were restricted, and we were concentrated in a area of about a quarter of a mile square. And every morning, the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] army came in to take Jews to labor for them, to wash, to clean, to clean the horses, to do all kinds of labor. And then another group came in-- the arbeitskommando for us to go out and work on the roads. Many times I was sitting on my knees and breaking stones into smaller-- approximately two to three inches size-- squares, which they used to fill the road in and build roads with. They were at this point preparing for an onslaught on the Soviet Union, the so-called Barbarossa project. This was early-- this was sort of early in 1941. And so we were to do that work for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people would-- would say lived in the ghetto when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say about 300 to 400. There were fewer every day because every night there was something, and every day was something, in which one or two or three or four lost their life in some bizarre ways, unexplained. Young girls, young men, old men-- it didn't matter. It was just the whim of the army. It was not even the SS. And so we come to the conclusion that it wasn't only the SS that was destroying the European Jewry. It was every German who lent his hand to function in behalf of the [? pogrom. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the ghetto surrounded by a barbed-wire fence?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Dobra ghetto was not surrounded by barbed wire. It was surround-- it-- it had a per se order that we cannot go out of this particular perimeter, but there was no fence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to wear anything identifying you as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and I still have some of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have to wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: A yellow Star of David with the word 'Jude' on if, and the letter spelling 'Jude' was sort of a abstract Hebrew letters, which was even more painful.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was this affixed to your clothing? Did you have to wear--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to attach it to the front in the left-hand side, approximate in the area of the heart, and in the rear, in the same area of the heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever try to leave the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister and I decided to go to Russia, decided to smuggle to Russia. And we decided one day, and we left very early in the morning. It was still dark. And we made it as far as the railroad station. At the railroad station we were caught as Jews, and we were told to go back and never do it again because the next time we'll be shot. And so we came back to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the food situation like inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the most difficult part because we were left sort of to a minimum of perhaps 300 or 400 or 500 calories a day in terms of what the Judenstaat could deliver to us. To substitute for the need that we were deprived of, we have traded our goods. We traded our personal things that we have. If anyone had some jewelry, he traded that to Poles that used to come to the ghetto or outside the ghetto in certain spots where they felt secure enough. And then we traded our clothing. We traded our bedding, and we traded everything that we had, and finally we had nothing to trade for. And the prices was staggering. Every day, a bread was going up enormously, and so that finally it wasn't any use to trade because you got really nothing for what you had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any type of rationing system?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The rationing system was very inefficient and very haphazard. Whatever they had, they give it to the Judenrat. And the Judenrat will deliver it to the ghetto, and then the ghetto, it would be distributed to each person. It would be announced that we come to pick up bread, and then we would get 300 to 400 grams of bread. Then we would get, well, some potatoes and some-- often some flour and some margarine and some jam and some accidental things.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Judenrat--

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Judenrat-- how did it function within the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] it was a very difficult role for the Judenrat. I know that the Judenrat is being remembered as one of the arms of the German Reich and fulfilling the Germans Reich's desire to destroy the Jews. But in a sense, one had to say that they had no choice. They were in a untenable position. They were put in a position that no person ever should be put in, where they had to squeeze-- literally squeeze-- the life out of every Jew in their own community and sometime deliver them to destruction, knowing that they're going to be destroyed. And so in 1941, early in May, the Judenrat got the order from Mr. Schweickert to deliver every Jewish male between the age of 16 and 60 for deportation to a labor camp, with the exception of one male that could remain with each family. My father [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] volunteered, and I went with him, to leave my older brother, who was in a position at that point to help my mother and my sister, we thought. But he too wasn't very long in the ghetto. He too was deported some eight or nine months later.

speaker: interviewer
text: At any point in the ghetto before you left, was there any attempt at any resistance, any underground with inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No good. No, there was-- we were so weak, so powerless, so unsupported from the outside, that we had nothing to resist with. There were no guns. As you know, in Europe, it's not allowed to for private people to have guns, especially at that time. Now it's perhaps much more liberal, but not those days, and there wasn't a single gun in any Jewish hand. Jews were not known to be fighters, were not known to-- to threaten with guns or need guns for whatever purpose they are used today for. So we had really nothing, nothing except hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any type of institution with inside the ghetto at this point, for example, a hospital or a school? Did any-- any institutions like that exist at all during the period that you were there inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no hospital in Dobra at all. There was no physician, no medical doctor in Dobra The nearest medical doctor was in Turek, which was some 15, 18 kilometers away from Dobra. There was nothing. There was a barber, so-called, a [? fetcher, ?] who was a glorified barber that did everything. He said [INAUDIBLE], and someone had pneumonia. And he gave him [? Baldrian ?] drops when he fainted, and these kind of homespun remedies. That's all there was available. There was nothing of that kind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when, actually, did you volunteer to leave? What-- what time of year was this now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm talking about 1941, May of 1941, middle of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was deported with my father. In a sense, I was hopeful that when they put us to work in a labor camp, they will have to support us with food if they want our labor. And maybe this is a rescue for my father and me, really, to be out of there. And of course, we had to say goodbye to our-- rest of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother came and brought me a little bundle with some instruments that I had, dental instruments for my first year of training, and insisted that I take those with me. And I said, Mom, what would I do with the instruments? I am not even a dentist. I don't know how to function with these instruments. I have so little rudimentary skill that I wouldn't be able to use them at all. And she says, please take them. Do me a favor. You know, like a Yiddish mama does. Please take them with you. You never know. And so I took these instruments with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what were the instruments?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were a couple of forceps, a couple of levers to extract teeth or extract roots from the teeth. There were a few escalator-- escalators. They were a few explorers, a few drills, a few-- whatever elementary needs I had in the first year of training. And so when I came to the first camp, to Steineck in the vicinity of Poznan, I kept these instruments under my pillow till someday someone was in need of a dentist. And there was no dentist. I was very fortunate. I was fortunate. I was lucky that there was no other person but I. And so I went in into the first aid room and sweating bullets my forehead and my back, I extracted his tooth. And so I became known as the dentist.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first camp, then, you were taken to was called--

speaker: interviewee
text: Steineck.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were transported by trucks, Louise, from Dobra to Turek, and in Turek, there were other trucks that joined us. And then in Kowal, which is another town north of Turek, there were other trucks that joined us. And when we came to the camp, we were a Kolonie of 16, 18 trucks. There were 1,040 people delivered to that one school house. This was in fenced. This was with guards, and this was with gates. And there were dogs that escorted us in a no-describable hostile manner to our barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe the barracks? What were they like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The barracks were-- we were in the school house and one school room. Which ordinarily was built for 40-- 30, 40 students at most-- we was 80 sleeping in this house, in this particular room. And our quarters that we slept on were simple shelves, just shelves, four stories high, four levels high. And as we were driven into this room for that my father and I were to be, we were trying to find a spot on these shelves. It was-- it was precious. Everyone ran to put his little rucksack that he had with him, this little satchel, so that he could claim this spot. And there was nothing on the higher shelf. We had to settle for the floor shelf. And that was the most undesirable spot to have because you had to go down on your knees. You had to bend down with your head in order to slide in. And you slid in on your neck and on your ankles, on your elbows, and on your knees. And so we made our home for a year in Steineck on this floor. And the-- soon we were plagued by vermin, by lice, by fleas, in such a manner that's hard to describe. We could-- in time, they grow so many of them that we could take them out in fists off the blankets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Steineck just a camp for men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just male, yeah. Just men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were brought-- excuse me-- to a Baustelle, which is workplace, where a German company by the name of [INAUDIBLE] was laying rails from west to east, and bogs, watered-- water-logged bogs which we had to dry out by bringing from in other areas sand and soil in order to fill it in. That was our work for the next year. I was assigned-- again fortunate. Fortunately, I was assigned to the kitchen because when I was transported, the administrator of the Jews to be delivered to Poznan-- his was Neumann, Dr. Neumann. And he saw me, and he called me for some reason. And I don't know why, but he called me. He asked my name, and he asked me what I do. And there wasn't time to explain. You couldn't take time to explain when you were called before a-- a Nazi, you knew that nothing good is coming out of. So he asked me what I do. I said, I'm a dentist. I didn't say I'm a student. I'm a dentist. So he says, when you come to camp, you report to me. And I feared what will happen, what he'll do to me. My father said, well, he won't remember you, even when he gets to camp. But anyhow, when we came to camp, he remembered me, and the following-- in the morning when we were supposed to go to work, he had already at that time chosen police. And one of the policemen by the name of Chaim Tszan, who was also a town-person of mine-- he called that the dentist should report to-- He didn't even know who he was calling, but he knew me. And he-- the dentist should report to Dr. Neumann. Pardon me. And I reported to Dr. Neumann, and Neumann put me aside. He put me in charge of the first group of 400 to go to this [GERMAN] to lead the Kolonie, so-called Kolonie, which is a group in German. And so I did. But when we came to the work place, they assigned each one to a different foreman. And I was assigned to the kitchen to bring water from a well, with another man. And that's what our work was. Shortly thereafter, I was chosen to work in the office for the [GERMAN], which was the overseer over the-- the foreman, the head foreman. And I kept hours for the people that came to work because they paid for us. [GERMAN] paid to the camp for our work on an hourly basis, which I didn't know but found out a little later. And I had to enter the hours when we came and how many we came each day and when we left, and summarized this, and multiply it, and so forth. And also do some work for the technician who was doing field-- field organizing at the time. So this was my work there. This was lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to fetch the water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Into the woods, just about a mile and a half away from the kitchen. It was a field kitchen, sort of like an army kitchen and open space, with two cauldrons where we were giving-- the kitchen-- was for us for soup. There was another kitchen inside the barracks that was for the foremen, food for the foremen. And so this man from Kowal by the name of Marek and I were lugging water from there. And on one of our trips, we suddenly heard [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] young female voices singing. And the sound came closer and closer and closer to us until it finally stopped. And they were singing a very well-known school song that almost everyone who went in Poland to school knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that song?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was something like, 'Today we are together, tomorrow we will be separated, and each one will go his own way,' and something, you know, along this line of what graduation of school has-- a graduation song, really. And so I sat, and I kept my eyes closed. I just was hoping that it wasn't a dream, that this was real, that it will repeat itself, that I hear the song again. But when I opened my eyes, there were five young ladies, young girls, looking at us. And inquisitively, they came over to us and asked who we were, what we doing here. This was the first labor camp in the region of Poznan. There was one camp that was established shortly before us, about a month before us, in Poznan itself, at the stadium. But this was outside Poznan, something like 20 kilometers outside Poznan. And they were not-- they have never seen-- Poznan wasn't very Jewish. In fact, these girls, except for one, didn't even know what a Jew was or have seen a Jew. They were small-town girls. And they worked in a-- in a nursery, in a garden nursery there. And they left with us. The [INAUDIBLE] that they had when they heard what we have gone through the last two days when we ordered inducted in this camp, and how we were treated, how badly, you know, what they are doing with us. And they took such pity on us that they brought us food every day, and we carried it back to the Baustelle, to the work place. And during lunch time, we divided it as often-- as much as we could among the inmates. However, one of them was very interested in me. And she asked me whether I can come back a little later. She would like to see me. And so we established a kind of a boy-and-girl relationship. She was about my age, perhaps a year or two younger. Her name was Zosia, Zosina, and she became the other fortunate that helped me survive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She followed me from camp to camp. She brought me food. She brought me medicine when I needed it. She even offered to hide me, should my father and I decide to escape. Until I came to Auschwitz. There, she could not see me anymore, nor did I see her after the war anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were able to leave the camp every day and meet with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were leaving the camp, and our walking time was approximately an hour. Was like six or eight kilometers from camp. And we were returning every night to camp. We had guards with us. They accompanied us to camp and back. They were Polish guards. They were not Germans. They were Polish. But they were in German service, sort of, like, you would say here-- who shall I say, any kind of a guard organization. The guards' houses and-- and businesses and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they know that you were meeting her?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the guards knew. The head guard knew. But he at this point was my friend. We worked out a deal with him. And he got some things from us, for which he allowed us on the way to work, running at dawn to a bakery and buy bread, come out, break it up into pieces so each one would have an extra supplement. Till one day, the commandant, Krusche, found out about our bread-buying project. And he called me in the first [? day ?] [? at ?] [? dawn, ?] and he made a football out of me. He beat me left and right. He just wanted to know who allowed me to walk out of the rows of the march, which guard. And I gave this head guard my word that no matter what, he'll never-- we will never tell him his name, that he will never be involved in this. And so he beat and beat and beat me till finally he saw that it makes no difference. And I felt it would make no difference even if I tell him who did it. Then he ordered the same policemen, Chaim Tszan, to meet out 21 lashes on my bare behind, had to lie over the [GERMAN], which was a wooden kind of a thing. And Tszan started to beat me. That wasn't the kind of lashing that he expected him to do, so he took the whip out of his hand, and he showed him how to do it. At that point, I lost consciousness. And the next time when I came to, I was in the hall on the cement. My father bent over me, and a few inmates, with water trying to resuscitate me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the guard? Do you remember the name of the guard that helped you out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, his name was Tadek. Tadeusz, was his real Polish name. But he was called Tadek, as most Polish names were sort of made into a K-- Marek, Tadek, Josek, Branek. I was called Branek.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so he knew of your meetings with Zosia?

speaker: interviewee
text: He knew of my meetings with Zosia, and he just closed his eyes to it. He never spoke with me about it, but I knew that he knows. Who knew more than he was the-- the-- Gospodini, which is the kitchen person that cooked for us. He cooked for the foreman. She knew about it. She sort of found out the second or third day. She saw it in my eyes. She says, I knew that there's something, that you staying there too long, and you're leaving in the afternoon. You're going over there. And be careful, you know, they will catch you. And if they catch you having any kind of relationship with a non-Jew, you know, you'll be shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was this-- you were in love?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in love, and I didn't care. I didn't care what will happen to me. I just dared so much in many other occasions that it is hard to explain.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop. We'll continue next tape.
